item.title: Scaling Terraform Across many Teams: A Native Framework for Platform Engineering
item.description: TL;DR: A pure Terraform framework that lets 50+ teams self-service infrastructure by...

item.title: How We Build Software with AI Assistants
item.description: From brainstorm to production in four phases — structured AI collaboration that produces better software than either alone.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Making SQE Work Everywhere: Pluggable Auth and Catalogs
item.description: How we're turning a single-vendor query engine into something that runs against any identity provider, any catalog, and any cloud.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: When Your SQL Engine Understands Meaning
item.description: SQL engines know table shapes. We're adding ontologies, property graphs, vector search, and AI-native interfaces.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: We Replaced Our Trino Fork with a Rust SQL Engine
item.description: How we went from maintaining a 2M-line Java fork to shipping a 50MB binary that runs every query as the authenticated user.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Building a Comprehensive SQL Benchmark Suite
item.description: Seven benchmark suites, 222 queries, and the infrastructure to measure performance honestly.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Bringing dbt to the Data Platform: A Browser-Based IDE
item.description: Integrating dbt Core into Chameleon with a full workspace IDE, git operations, lineage visualization, and AI-assisted model development.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: AI Flows: Langflow as the Platform's Intelligence Layer
item.description: Embedding Langflow as a managed AI service with auto-switching flows, MCP tool integration, and context-aware assistance.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: A Visual Editor for Open Data Contracts
item.description: A full ODCS v3.1.0 contract editor with tabbed forms, live YAML preview, catalog import with AI-suggested quality checks, and dbt export.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Docker Dev Mode: Hot Reload for the Full Stack
item.description: A docker-compose.dev.yml overlay for instant feedback on frontend and backend changes without rebuilding containers.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Databricks-Style Column Profiling in the Data Explorer
item.description: Inline column statistics with distribution sparklines computed from real SQL queries, not just Iceberg manifest metadata.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Dropping Radix UI: Going Fully Custom for React 19
item.description: How a React 19 infinite re-render loop led us to replace all third-party UI primitives with 660 lines of custom components.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: From 63% to 95%: Building Trino SQL Compatibility in a Single Day
item.description: Implementing 70+ UDFs, Iceberg time travel, metadata TVFs, and engine-level SQL features for Trino drop-in replacement.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Streaming Writes, Sort Order Safety, and the IN (Subquery) Workaround
item.description: Fixing OOM in CTAS, safe Iceberg sort order for mixed writers, and working around DataFusion limitations.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Five Layers of Caching and an 8.8x Speedup Over Trino
item.description: How multi-layer caching took SQE from slower than Trino to 2.5-8.8x faster across every benchmark suite.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: How Agentic AI Helped Us Beat Trino
item.description: 221 queries, 7 suites, one week — how an AI assistant running automated benchmarks drove a major performance breakthrough.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Auditing a Data Platform with AI: Iterative Security Hardening
item.description: Four audit rounds with escalating AI personas found 42 security issues and removed 6,500 lines of dead code.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Benchmark Data Generation: TPC at the Click of a Button
item.description: An admin page that generates TPC benchmark datasets on-demand. Pick a benchmark, pick a scale, click Generate — sqe-bench does the heavy lifting in a Docker container while SQE loads the results into Iceberg tables under your identity.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: 43 Findings, Zero Deferred: A Production Security Audit of a Rust SQL Engine
item.description: We ran a full production sign-off audit against SQE and found 43 issues across security, runtime safety, logic bugs, and code quality. Then we fixed all of them in one session.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: When the Ground Shifts Faster Than People Can Stand
item.description: A companion piece to The Human Side of the Machine Shift — exploring why AI disruption hits different minds differently, and how leadership can turn discomfort into team strength.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: DataFusion 53, a Vendored Fork, and 40% Faster Queries
item.description: We upgraded SQE from DataFusion 52 to 53 by forking and rebasing iceberg-rust ourselves. The result: 27-40% faster across every benchmark suite.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Our Nemesis: TPC-DS Query 72 and the Limits of a Custom SQL Engine
item.description: One query. Ten tables. Twelve times slower than Trino. Everything we tried, what worked, what didn't, and where the ceiling is.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The Iceberg Matrix and the Quiet Bug Hiding in V3
item.description: We thought the V3 path worked. The unit tests said it worked. The matrix called it 'partial' and we agreed. Then we wrote eleven end-to-end tests and discovered Polaris had been silently rejecting every V3 column type for months.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: SQE Talks to Five Catalogs Now: HMS, Nessie, Glue, JDBC, S3 Tables
item.description: We claimed the engine was catalog-agnostic. Time to prove it. One branch, five live integration tests, one small AWS SigV4 patch, and a matrix score that moved from 153 to 158.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Why a Public Iceberg Matrix Beats Vendor Spec Sheets
item.description: Sixty-three capabilities, three levels, no marketing. The Iceberg Matrix is what compatibility looks like when the rubric is public and the evidence has to land in code. Here is why it works, what we learned from sitting on it, and why every open standard needs one.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: How we accidentally created a DuckDB
item.description: SQE started as a distributed Iceberg query engine. Five MRs later it queries CSVs from disk, Parquet from S3, and Parquet from HuggingFace. We did not plan that.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: One Binary, No Cluster: SQE Goes Embedded
item.description: We built SQE for distributed Iceberg, but most of the time you just want to look at a parquet file. Here's how we made the engine work both ways without forking the codebase.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Shipping OpenLineage: column-level lineage for an Iceberg engine
item.description: SQE now emits OL 2-0-2 events with column-level lineage on every write. Here is what they look like, why we walked the LogicalPlan to build them, and the disk spool we did not want to write.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Mounting catalogs from SQL: ATTACH, DETACH, and the registry pattern
item.description: SQE now ships DuckDB-style ATTACH / DETACH and CREATE / DROP SECRET. The story of building it covers parser extension, credential hygiene, a lifecycle bug we found in the integration tests, and a state-store pattern that is starting to repeat.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Nine PRs, two merge conflicts, and the value of themed branches
item.description: Sunday afternoon: an audit dropped eighteen issues into the tracker. Monday morning we had nine merge requests open. Two of them hit conflicts on rebase. Neither one mattered. Here is why that was deliberate, not lucky.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: read_parquet shouldn't read /etc/shadow
item.description: Modern object-store abstractions unify the filesystem and HTTP behind a single URL. That's a feature for ergonomics. It's a security trap when the URL comes from a user. SQE shipped that trap and then closed it. The IMDS pivot is the part worth telling.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: npm install Shouldn't Run Your Code
item.description: Supply chain attacks on package registries are surging across every ecosystem. Two controls block the majority: a quarantine window and disabled install scripts. The exact config for npm, pip, Go, Maven, NuGet, Cargo, and more — plus a one-command proxy and shell tests you can run today.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Nineteen MRs, four waves, and the failure modes of agent batches at scale
item.description: Two days after the nine-PR audit pass, we ran a bigger one. 130 issues filed, 19 themed MRs merged across four waves. Same workflow, more failure modes. Watchdog stalls, a reboot mid-wave, a broken main, and config.rs as the conflict magnet. Here is what actually happened.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: q72, our nemesis, and the Int32 that hid for a month
item.description: TPC-DS q72 sat at 10 seconds while every other query ran in under 1.4. Five days of investigation chased scan parallelism, range-based NDV, iceberg-rust upgrades, and the RisingWave fork. None of those were the bug. The bug was a silently-skipped Err arm in our dynamic-filter evaluator that swallowed every Int32 vs Int64 type clash. Fixing it: 15.5s to 0.77s. q72 now beats Trino.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The SSB regression that wasn't
item.description: MR #220 wired runtime filters into iceberg-rust's scan path and dropped TPC-DS 67%. SSB looked like it regressed 6%. Two failed heuristic attempts, one parquet-trace session, and ten warm passes later, the regression turned out to be measurement noise. The fix-the-fix that wasn't, and the data-clustering insight that explains why two suites with the same code path behave nothing alike.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Porting DuckDB's BinarySerializer to pure Rust
item.description: Ten sub-MRs in a day. The wire format, the fixture-driven debugging loop, and two bugs that the C++ reference encoder ships without telling you: WriteListWithDefault elision, and uninitialised bytes at NULL VARCHAR positions.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The Last Mile Problem: Why 100% Supply Chain Coverage is Hard
item.description: Every time a developer runs npm install, they're implicitly trusting hundreds of strangers. Intercepting that trust is straightforward in theory — and surprisingly subtle in practice.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Speaking Quack: SQE as a DuckDB server, a DuckDB client, and a federation engine
item.description: DuckDB 1.5 ships a wire protocol called Quack. We re-implemented it in pure Rust, turned SQE into both server and client, and proved you can JOIN an Iceberg table with a remote DuckDB table in a single SELECT. Two waves of bugs, one federated query.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The type matrix as a roadmap: seven DuckDB types in two days
item.description: We started with a markdown table tracking which DuckDB types we could round-trip. The table became the roadmap. DECIMAL, LIST, STRUCT, MAP, ARRAY, ENUM, UNION each got their own MR. The surprises came from how DuckDB models the relationships: MAP is LIST<STRUCT>, UNION is STRUCT with a tag field, and DECIMAL packs four widths into one logical type.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Renovate + Supply Chain: What It Actually Intercepts (and What It Doesn't)
item.description: We tested Renovate's registry behaviour for every ecosystem — npm, PyPI, Go, Cargo, Maven, NuGet, Composer — and found that minimumReleaseAge works for all of them, but how each datasource resolves its registry is surprisingly different. Here's what we found and how to build a complete defence.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Supply Chain Attacks Hit Every Ecosystem. Here's How to Defend Yours.
item.description: A three-layer defense for developers and DevOps engineers: harden your dev machine in 5 minutes, lock down project configs for every ecosystem, and enforce policy org-wide with a proxy. Covers npm, pnpm, yarn, bun, pip, uv, Go, Maven, Gradle, NuGet, Cargo, and Composer — plus escrow, JFrog Curation, and Nexus.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Data Quality That Lives in a Contract
item.description: Data quality belongs in the table's contract, not scattered across dbt tests and cron jobs. We build on the Open Data Contract Standard, compile quality rules to portable SQL, and run them on the live engine under your identity — plus an honest accounting of what's wired and what isn't.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: dbt, Built Into the Platform
item.description: Not shelling out to the dbt CLI and calling it integration — a browser-native dbt IDE with git-backed projects, container runs, column-level lineage, and one-click quick-test. How a project becomes a workspace, and where the regex shortcuts bite.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: An Ontology You Can Query: From a Blank Canvas to a Knowledge Graph
item.description: A semantic layer that doesn't end in a triple store. The schema lives in Postgres, the data stays in Iceberg, and one query translator compiles every façade — MCP, GraphQL, SQL views — down to the same SQL.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Setting It All Up With Terraform
item.description: A Terraform provider for the platform's own resources — warehouses, namespaces, grants, dbt projects, contracts, ontologies — managed in HCL, in a repo, in a pull request, with a plan you can read before you apply.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: A Data Platform You Actually Own
item.description: Every managed data platform asks you to put your data, identities, and queries in the vendor's plane. This one doesn't. Data, identity, catalog, compute, and policy — all five in infrastructure you control, by construction.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: How It Compares: Databricks, Snowflake, iomete
item.description: An honest read of where this platform sits against Databricks, Snowflake, and iomete — on the one axis that decides the choice: how much control you keep versus how much operation you hand off. Including where we lose.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: What the Data Platform Actually Does
item.description: The map of the platform: an assembly of open-source parts behind a Backend-for-Frontend that holds your token and enforces your identity at every hop. What each surface does, how a request flows, and where the cliffs are.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: A dashboard for the engine
item.description: SQE already tracked every query and worker in memory, but there was no way to look at it without tailing logs or wiring Grafana. So we put a read-only dashboard on the health port: no login, no build step, no external assets. One HTML page over a small JSON API, with a tiny in-memory sampler so the charts move over time. Here is how it is built and why it stays deliberately small.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Two Tenants in One Apply: Workspaces as Code
item.description: Multi-tenancy on a data platform is usually a ticket and a week of console clicking. We made it a Terraform apply: a workspace, its catalog, its groups, its grants, and its dbt project — isolated by construction, reviewed in a pull request.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Lake Formation gates the catalog, not the rows
item.description: Our own README promised fine-grained Lake Formation in the SQE Glue quickstart. The engine does not do it. Here is what Lake Formation actually enforces when SQE reads S3 directly, and where SQE's real column and row masking lives.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Runnable docs, or how the quickstarts became a test suite
item.description: We turned every 'how to run SQE for X' into a self-contained run.sh that goes from clean state to captured output. The point was documentation. The payoff was a test suite that caught a missing metric, an overclaimed capability, and a benchmark that would have polluted our baselines.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: columns(2) must match fields(16)
item.description: Differential testing against Trino caught a distributed-scan bug the whole test suite missed: every projected query failed once a scan actually distributed. The first fix restored correctness by disabling projection pushdown. Then a Claude agent on the Fable model found the real bug, one line in the worker's streaming rewrite, and got the speed back: 3.1x overall, 10x on the worst query.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The benchmark that lied, the oracle that didn't, and the day Trino was wrong
item.description: Our SF0.1 compare run looked great: zero mismatches across seven suites. Then we asked DuckDB to check the data and found that 16 'passing' TPC-DS queries had never selected a single row, TPC-C had zero warehouses, and the one real disagreement between SQE and Trino was Trino's fault. Plus: the dynamic filter that shipped 6 million rows because nobody carried it across a node swap.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: One file, one thread, and the 910ms that explained the SSB gap
item.description: On SSB at SF1 Trino ran scan-heavy queries about 2x faster than SQE, even though we pruned just as well. A new line in our query profile found it: a 151MB lineorder file decoded on a single thread, 94% of a 969ms query spent waiting on one scan. The obvious fix (more partitions) was a trap that regressed q72 from 17s to 100s once before. The safe fix parallelizes decode inside the scan without changing the plan the optimizer sees.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Six groups where the spec allows four
item.description: TPC-H q01 returned six (returnflag, linestatus) combinations on our generated data. The spec defines exactly four. Both SQE and Trino agreed on the wrong answer, because both read the same broken tables. The bug class behind it: fields the spec derives from other fields, drawn instead as independent uniform random. Five defects in TPC-H, one in SSB, all the same shape.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The filter that rebuilt itself 14,600 times
item.description: A two-table TPC-H join that Trino ran in 2.2s took us 161s at SF10. We blamed partition layout, then single-node joins, then a subquery pattern. All three were wrong. A CPU profile and two timers found the truth: a runtime filter we pushed to the probe scan was getting re-snapshotted once per batch, and each snapshot rebuilt a 300,000-node expression tree. The fix snapshots it once. q12 went 161s to 2.7s, q17 176s to 7.1s, q10 from a 300s failure to 3.3s, with the result rows unchanged and no knob touched.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: DataFusion 54: what it actually unblocked
item.description: DataFusion 54 landed and we bumped to it. The port was mostly mechanical, with one real behavioral change in the shuffle hasher. The interesting part is what the release notes implied and the engine did not deliver: LATERAL joins are logical-plan only, array lambdas still fail, and the one compatibility win we found was a documentation bug, not a DataFusion feature. We tested every claim before we wrote it down.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: When GRANT becomes a Ranger policy
item.description: SQE got a second access-control backend: write GRANT/REVOKE as Apache Ranger policy and let Polaris enforce it. The protocol took an afternoon. The identity model took the week, because Polaris federation does not work the way the token suggests.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: One mask, and Spark and SQE agree to the byte
item.description: SQE's fine-grained Ranger backend does row filters, column masks, role-conditional masking, and tags. The kicker: the same Ranger policy on the same Polaris catalog produces byte-exact identical masked output in SQE and in standard Apache Spark. We proved it live, and the first run failed for a reason no amount of reading could have predicted.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Snowflake's governance model on open Iceberg
item.description: Snowflake gives you masking policies, row access policies, object tags, and a GRANT model. SQE gives you the same primitives on Apache Ranger and open Iceberg, enforced by plan rewrite and shared across engines. Here is the mapping, the one real edge SQE has, and the gaps we have not closed yet.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The log bucket that finally takes KMS
item.description: S3 server access logs could only land in an AES256 bucket for years, and AES256 is exactly what Security Hub flags. The escape was never a bucket setting. It was a KMS key policy that lets the logging service write with your key.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: AWS Community Builder
item.description: Part of the AWS Community Builders Program
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: AWS Glue vulnerabilities in default packages
item.description: Securing AWS Glue: A Guide to Identifying and Fixing Python Package Vulnerabilities ...

item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: AWSUG.nl talk about Step Functions
item.description: AWSUG.nl Communityday talk about Step Functions with the overview of the released features
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Redesigning This Blog (While Writing About It)
item.description: A meta-journey through redesigning a blog's layout - avoiding AI design clichés and building something with actual character
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Bridging Clouds: A Guide to Connecting AWS Glue Tables with Snowflake
item.description: In today's data-driven world, organizations often find themselves working with multiple cloud...

item.title: CDK Day 2022
item.description: CDK Day 26 May 2022
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Collibra Protect , Snowflake and Iceberg tables
item.description: Iceberg is gaining traction, and Collibra is expanding its presence as a data governance tool,...

item.title: Data Engineering and ChatGPT (Part2)
item.description: In my previous post i must have hit some issue with ChatGPT. It's way more impressive then i...

item.title: Data Engineering and ChatGPT
item.description: ChatGPT is out and everybody is trying it. Blogs, songs are easy, but can it help with Data...

item.title: Docker on Mac with Colima
item.description: Use the free alternative Colima to run docker on mac
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Duckberg!
item.description: I wrote a previous small blog about PyIceberg and Glue iceberg Rest Api This week is saw the...

item.title: DUCKDB, S3 Tables with iceberg using Iceberg Rest API
item.description: I wrote my previous article about Duckberg, a combination of PyIceberg with Duckdb to access iceberg...

item.title: Efficient Agentic AI Development Guide (begin 2026)
item.description: A practical guide for working effectively with AI coding agents, especially Claude Code as of begin...

item.description: Python and modules for ETL jobs on AWS
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: From Dark Flow to Real Momentum: Why Claude Opus 4.6 Feels Like an Extension of Me
item.description: The recent article from fast.ai, titled "Breaking the Spell of Vibe Coding" by Rachel Thomas...

item.title: From Extension to Orchestration: Who Wins (and Who Gets Left Behind) in the Claude 4.6 Era
item.description: I published this morning "From Dark Flow to Real Momentum: Why Claude Opus 4.6 Feels Like an...

item.title: Glue Iceberg Rest Api and PyIceberg
item.description: Access Glue Iceberg tables via the Iceberg Rest Api AWS Released silently Iceberg REST-API...

item.description: Glue SBOM exporter and inspector
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.description: Using external python modules in AWS Glue
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Glue SBOM exporter and vulnerabilities
item.description: Glue SBOM exporter and vulnerabilities
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: I Stopped Maintaining Terraform Examples and Tests Separately. Here's Why.
item.description: TL;DR: Every time you update a Terraform example, you should also update its corresponding test. Stop...

item.title: Glue Iceberg Rest Api and PyIceberg
item.description: Using the glue iceberg rest api with pyiceberg
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Unity Iceberg Rest Api and PyIceberg
item.description: Using the Unity iceberg rest api with pyiceberg
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Make your Jupyter Notebook/IPython full width
item.description: Make your Jupyter Notebook/IPython full width
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule
item.description: Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule and why they don't combine.
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: New EC2 Models re:invent 2022
item.description: AWS Released 5 new EC2 models in Re:invent 2022. Hereby an...

item.title: An ON-AIR sign with IoT
item.description: Building an sign with iot to show when you are in a meeting"
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: OpenClaw: The Open-Source Agent That Feels Too Alive
item.description: It hits like the moment teenagers first logged onto BBS boards or IRC channels in the late '80s and...

item.title: Overview of the features released for Step Functions
item.description: At the Dutch AWS Community Day 2022, i held the following talk. An overview of all the features...

item.title: Purge a glue table from cli
item.description: Sometimes you want to purge a glue table from s3 and delete all files and versions. Lately i had to...

item.title: Re:invent 2022 Releases sorted
item.description: Re:invent 2022 Releases sorted
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Re:invent 2022 Releases sorted
item.description: An overview of all the releases sorted per group. This re:invent was very focused on Data &...

item.title: Re:invent 2023 News
item.description: reinvent 23 news
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: The Database Evolution: Breaking Free from Monolithic Thinking
item.description: A technical analysis of AWS's latest database innovations, from Aurora's Graviton4 support to OpenSearch Serverless improvements. Learn how these changes are reshaping data architecture patterns and enabling more efficient, distributed database systems with real-world implementation strategies.
item.author: Cloud Architect's Perspective

item.title: The Silent Revolution in Disaster Recovery: How AWS is Changing the Game
item.description: Explore how AWS's new zonal shift capabilities and Security Incident Response features are transforming traditional disaster recovery. This technical deep-dive reveals how automatic failover, self-healing systems, and integrated security responses are making DR more reliable and cost-effective than ever before.
item.author: Cloud Architect's Perspective

item.title: The Human Touch in Digital Transformation: Beyond Just Technology
item.description: An architect's perspective on AWS's latest customer experience innovations, including Amazon Connect's AI capabilities and Polly's new synthetic voices. Discover how these technologies are making digital interactions more natural while improving efficiency and customer satisfaction.
item.author: Cloud Architect's Perspective

item.title: Infrastructure Efficiency: The Hidden Environmental Impact of AWS's Latest Announcements
item.description: Discover how AWS's latest infrastructure updates, particularly the expansion of Graviton4 processors and R8g instances, are quietly revolutionizing cloud computing sustainability. Learn about real-world performance improvements, cost reductions, and environmental benefits of these strategic changes.
item.author: Cloud Architect's Perspective

item.title: Re:invent 2024 News
item.description: reinvent 24 news
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: AWS Sagemaker Canvas Remove
item.description: How to remove AWS Sagemaker Canvas
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Secrets and AWS GLUE Custom Connectors
item.description: For a project i had to retrieve data from Teradata using a Glue Job. A quick google gave me this:...

item.title: Setting Up IOMete: A Cloud-Independent Data Platform Based on Spark
item.description: IOMete is a powerful, cloud-independent data platform built on Apache Spark, designed to enable...

item.title: Sustainability on AWS
item.description: Sustainability is the new topic on AWS. While it has a lot in common with FinOps, it adds emphasis to...

item.title: The AI Development Workflow: A Complete System for Working with AI Agents
item.description: A continuous cycle of ideation, planning, execution, and refinement — all driven by issues, feedback...

item.title: The Art of Agents: Sun Tzu's Principles for Building Agentic AI Systems
item.description: By Jacob Verhoeks, March 2026 The cost of building software has collapsed. Claude Code ships...

item.title: The Hidden Dangers in Our Software Supply Chain: Why It's Bigger Than You Think
item.description: In today's fast-paced digital world, software powers everything from the apps on our phones to the...

item.title: The Review Bottleneck: Rethinking Software and Infrastructure Design for the Agent Era
item.description: AI coding agents made code generation cheap. They did not make software delivery cheap. Teams...

item.title: Unity Catalog Iceberg Rest Api and PyIceberg
item.description: Access Unity tables via the Iceberg Rest Api After working with glue catalog in the...

item.title: Votes and Views Part 1
item.description: Votes and Views Part 1
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Autocomplete IAM in vscode
item.description: Autocomplete IAM in vscode
item.author: Jacob Verhoeks

item.title: Why Debian packages are safer then NPM and PyPi
item.description: In the world of software development, package repositories are critical for distributing libraries...

item.title: Your Terraform Examples Are Broken (And You Don't Know It Yet)
item.description: TL;DR: Stop maintaining separate examples and tests. Test your examples directly. One source of...
